summary: Fowler identifies three distinct modes of using UML: as sketch, as blueprint, and as programming language. He argues that disagreements about what UML should contain stem from these different fundamental perspectives on its purpose. UML 2's changes toward greater precision serve blueprint and programming language users but burden sketchers with unnecessary complexity. Fowler predicts the UML will diverge into a formal standard and informal conventional usage, with the informal variants potentially fragmenting further as they drift from the standard.
key_points: People disagree about UML because they have fundamentally different views of its purpose - sketch, blueprint, or programming language
Your perception of UML is shaped by which mode you were first introduced to
UML 2's precision-focused changes serve formal users but make it harder for sketchers
The informal 'sketch' usage of UML is already diverging from the standard in non-UML books
This divergence risks fragmenting informal UML usage as it drifts further from the standard
money_quotes: People differ about what should be in the UML because there are differing fundamental views about what the UML should be.
When someone else's view of the UML seems rather different to yours, it may be because they use a different mode to you.
Your perception of the UML may be affected by the mode in which it was introduced to you.
These changes increase the bulk of the UML - and thus make it harder for those who prefer UmlAsSketch.
The UML may well diverge into similar but different things: the standard UML and a conventional, informal usage for the sketchers.
key_insight: Tools and notations mean different things to different users based on how they use them, and adding precision for one group's needs can burden others.
